doctrinal_renderings.advaita: According to Shankara, the triple impulse to action (karma-codana) — jnana (the act of knowing), jneya (the object known), and parijnata (the apparent knower, the bhoktru posited by avidya through upadhi-superimposition) — arises entirely within the domain of avidya and has no purchase on the kutastha atman. The triple summary of action (karma-sangraha) — karana (instruments: external senses such as the ear, internal faculties such as buddhi), karma (the most-desired object of the agent), and karta (the instrument-wielder, himself only an upadhi-coloring) — likewise belongs wholly to the gunas. Since the atman is witness and not agent, it is untouched by all this threefold machinery; what the ignorant call 'I act' is only the upadhi-constructed self revolving within its own appearance.
doctrinal_renderings.viśiṣṭādvaita: Ramanuja reads the threefold karma-codana as the formal structure of Vedic injunction: jnana is the knowledge of what duty is to be performed, jneya is that duty itself (typified by jyotishtoma and the like), and parijnata is the qualified aspirant who comprehends it — together these constitute the boda-boddha-boddhri (knowledge-object-knower) triad that makes a Vedic vidhi operative. The threefold karma-sangraha — karana (material means and accessories), karma (the sacrifice itself), and karta (the one who undertakes it) — is thus not a trap but the very architecture through which the jiva's loving service (kainkarya) to Bhagavan is discharged. Detached, Bhagavat-dedicated action within this structure IS bhakti-yoga in its preparatory form.
doctrinal_renderings.dvaita: Madhva insists that the triple karma-codana does not imply the jiva is an independent agent; on the contrary, it is precisely because these three conditions obtain that Hari's prescriptive will (vidhi) operates through them. The karma-sangraha (karana, karma, karta) compresses the five-factor analysis — the adhishthana and the rest are subsumed within karana — and Madhva cites a Rigveda-khila verse confirming this. Even though the jiva is in reality akarta (non-independent doer), the vidhi produces real desire and effort through Ishvara's inward prompting (iccha-utpatti), and the result is fixed by Him alone; the appearance of autonomous kartritva is only abhimanika (superimposed self-attribution), relative to inert matter.
doctrinal_renderings.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha situates this verse within the central Pushti-marga thesis that all variation in the world arises from sattva-vriddhi (the increase of sattva) as Bhagavan's spontaneous prasada. The triple karma-codana — jneya as the sadhana-object, the kartavya karma, and the parijnata who knows it — is the form the tri-guna karma-vidhi takes in the Veda, fulfilling the principle 'traigunya-vishaya vedah' (2.45). The karma-sangraha likewise — karana (instruments including indriya and dravya), karma (yajna and the like), karta (the performer) — is tri-guna in nature; but for the devotee on Pushti-marga this entire machinery is Krishna's lila-rupa, received as gift, not self-constructed.
doctrinal_renderings.bhakti: Shridhara reads this verse as grounding the claim that the atman 'neither slays nor is bound' (BG 18.17): because both karma-codana and karma-sangraha are tri-guna in nature, the nirguna atman has no real contact with them. The triple codana — jnana as the cognition 'this means is fit,' jneya as that means (the kartavya action), and parijnata as the locus of such cognition — together constitute the impulse that sets karma in motion. The three-member karma-sangraha is the locus of karma itself: karana (sadhaka-tama, the most efficacious instrument), karma (the most desired object of the agent), karta (the kriya-nirvartaka). Shridhara flags, following the Mimaṃsaka formula cited also by Vallabha, that codana, upadesa, and vidhi are co-referential — the verse thus also encodes how the Veda's prescriptive voice operates through gunas, never touching the gunatita atman.
doctrinal_renderings.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudana Sarasvati offers the most architecturally dense reading: building on the five-factor analysis of BG 18.13-14, he now re-describes the same ground through a jnana-jneya-parijnata analysis to show that the codana (impulse) and the karma-sangraha (locus of action) are both kararaka-rupa, and since karakas are tri-guna, the kutastha atman remains asparsha (untouched) by all action. Karana comprises outer sense-organs and inner antahkarana (buddhi etc.); karma is the vivarta-vikara-apya-samskarya object; karta is the cid-acid-granthi (the knot of consciousness and matter) that wields all instruments. He then pivots to an alternative reading: jnana is the prescriptive awareness generated by Vedic ling-adi forms, jneya is the injunctive word itself as its object, parijnata is the promisee — making codana equivalent to the Mimamsaka shabda-bhavana theory of Vedic injunction — only to conclude that since both bhavana-traya (the semantic machinery of injunction) and karaka-shatka (the six case-roles) are tri-guna, the Veda's reach is exhausted at the guna-level and cannot touch the nistraigunya atman.

so_what_questions: If every impulse to act (jnana, jneya, parijnata) is itself constituted by the gunas, can the decision to renounce action be any less guna-determined — or does the verse quietly dissolve the agent who would renounce?

Shankara and Ramanuja both affirm the three-member structure, yet one uses it to dissolve the agent and the other to oblige the agent toward Bhagavan. What is at stake in whether the karta is an upadhi-fiction or a real qualified jiva?

The verse pairs karma-codana (the impulse) with karma-sangraha (the locus). What happens when the impulse is present but the locus is missing — for instance, in dreaming or in samadhi? Does karma arise without a karana?
Madhva's reading preserves both divine vidhi and jiva akartritva simultaneously. Is this coherent, or does it smuggle back an autonomous jiva through the back door of 'abhimanika kartritva'?
If codana, upadesa, and vidhi are all synonyms (Bhatta formula), does the Gita's own prescriptive speech act here turn on itself — is BG 18.18 itself a codana whose operation is also tri-guna?

The bhakti school (Shridhara) uses tri-guna pervasiveness to demonstrate nirguna atman's non-contact with action. The advaita-bhakti school (Madhusudana) uses the same argument to validate traiguṇya-vishaya vedah. Are these compatible conclusions or rival uses of the same premise?

Modern cognitive science maps jnana onto representation, jneya onto affordance, and parijnata onto the predictive self-model. Does this mapping illuminate, flatten, or category-error the verse's analytic?

everyday_applications.advaita: Before beginning any project, notice the three-part structure already in motion: the knowing ('I understand the task'), the known object ('this is what must be done'), and the apparent knower ('I am the one who will do it'). Shankara's point is that this entire structure is assembled by conditioning, not by an independent self. Catching it assembling itself — without trying to stop it — is the praktica equivalent of jnana-yoga in daily work.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Ramanuja's reading invites a professional reframe: the knowledge of what needs doing, the task itself, and you-the-qualified-doer are not a burden but a gift that allows kainkarya (loving service). Each morning's brief review of 'what is required of me today, by whom, with what means' is not managerial routine but the Vedic vidhi structure operating in you — so bring it the same reverence you would a ritual.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Madhva's insight applies to creative blocks: the feeling 'I cannot act' or 'I am not the real author of this' is not nihilism but precision. Your kartritva is abhimanika — real enough for accountability, hollow enough that you need not carry the full weight of outcomes. Act through the vidhi (the task structure before you) and let Hari carry the result.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's prasada-lens: the very fact that right now you have the knowledge, the object of action, and the capacity to act is not your achievement — it is sattva as prasada. Treat each aligned moment ('I know what to do, I have what I need, I am able') as a gift arriving, not a resource you built. Gratitude precedes effort.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Shridhara's layering of codana as both psychological impulse and Vedic injunction suggests: when you feel genuinely moved to act rightly, do not second-guess it as 'mere emotion.' The impulse, the object, and the capacity converging at once is the tri-guna machinery serving its function. Ride it cleanly, without attachment to being its author.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudana's cid-acid-granthi (the knot of awareness and matter) is the practical touchpoint: in any moment of decision, you are neither pure witness nor pure body — you are the knot. Recognizing this prevents both the error of over-claiming agency ('I alone did this') and the error of spiritual bypass ('nothing is really happening'). Work from the knot, aimed at untying it.

self_assessment: All six bhashyas present and substantive; Shridhara payload clean Sanskrit prose; doctrinal divergence genuine across agent-dissolution (Shankara), service-structure (Ramanuja), divine-dependence (Madhva), prasada-receipt (Vallabha), nirguna-non-contact (Shridhara), and shabda-bhavana synthesis (Madhusudana).
